segments.purgatorio-30-g72: Ben is the adverb 'well' (or 'indeed') modifying the verb; son is an archaic/poetic short form of sono (first-person singular of essere) meaning 'I am.' The phrase is a clipped, emphatic statement ('I am well') with the subject pronoun omitted because it is expressed by the verb ending.
segments.purgatorio-30-g73: Ben again is the adverb 'well' (here introducing the second clause); son is the same first-person singular 'I am'; Beatrice is a proper name in the nominative used as the predicate noun after the verb ('I am Beatrice'). The sequence identifies the speaker.
segments.purgatorio-30-g74: Come is the interrogative 'how.' Degnasti is the second-person singular past form (an older/past tense of degnare, here 'you deigned') used rhetorically. D’ is the contraction of di before a vowel and introduces the following infinitive; accedere is the infinitive 'to approach/come to/enter'; al is the contraction of a + il 'to the'; monte is the noun 'mountain.' The whole string is an archaically phrased negative/expectant question: 'How did you deign to come to the mountain?'
segments.purgatorio-30-g75: Non is the negative particle 'not.' Sapei is an archaic/poetic 2nd-person verb form used rhetorically here (roughly 'did you know' or 'didn't you know'); tu is the emphatic subject 'you.' Che introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; qui means 'here'; è is the 3rd-person singular present of essere 'is'; l’ is the contracted definite article 'the'; uom (short for uomo) is 'man' (here meaning 'person' or 'man' in general); felice means 'happy' or 'fortunate' (often 'blessed' in Dante). Together it asks—often with surprised reproach—whether the addressee knew that here stands the happy/fortunate man.
segments.purgatorio-30-g76: A punctuation mark (full stop) marking the end of the preceding utterance/sentence.
segments.purgatorio-30-g47: 'Ma' is the adversative 'but'. 'n’avea' is a contracted poetic form of 'ne aveva' (imperfect of avere) and here combines with the past participle 'lasciati' (agrees plural) to form a compound past meaning 'had left (them)'. 'scemi' is a predicate adjective (plural) meaning 'stunned/bereft' and 'di sé' ('of himself') indicates what they are deprived of; overall: 'but Virgil had left them stunned/deprived of himself.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g48: A short apposition/vocative: the name 'Virgilio' followed by the superlative adjective 'dolcissimo' ('most sweet, most dear') and the noun 'patre' ('father'). The verb is omitted (apostrophic address), so this serves as an emphatic reprise of the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-30-g49: 'a cui' = 'to whom' (relative phrase referring back to Virgil). 'per mia salute' = 'for my salvation/health'. 'die’mi' is an elided/poetic past form (die' = diede) with the enclitic 'mi' attached, i.e. 'gave me' — so the clause means 'to whom (he) gave me for my salvation' or 'who gave himself/acted for my salvation' depending on context; the clitic order (verb+mi) is a poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-30-g50: 'né' = 'nor'. 'quantunque' is concessive ('however much' / 'although'). 'perdeo' is an archaic/poetic verb form of 'perdere' (here meaning 'I lost' or 'I had lost'); 'l’antica matre' = 'the ancient mother' as the direct object. The whole segment reads concessively: 'nor, however much I had lost the ancient mother...'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g51: 'valse' is the third-person singular past of 'valere' meaning 'it availed' or 'it was of use'. The phrase 'a le guance nette di rugiada' is the indirect object: 'to/for the cheeks pure with dew' (a poetic image for tear-wetted cheeks). So: 'it availed to the dew-clean cheeks...' linking back to the concessive clause.
segments.purgatorio-30-g52: Relative pronoun 'che' introducing the subordinate clause that modifies 'guance' (the cheeks): 'which/that...'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g53: 'lagrimando' is the present participle (or gerund) of 'lagrimare' meaning 'weeping' and functions adverbially to describe the manner of the action in the relative clause ('that, weeping,...').
segments.purgatorio-30-g54: 'non' negates the clause. 'tornasser' is an archaic imperfect subjunctive/conditional form (modern 'tornassero' or 'tornassero') of 'tornare' meaning 'would return' or 'should return'; 'atre' is an adjective 'black/dark'. Together: 'that, weeping, would not turn dark' (i.e. they would not be made dark again).
segments.purgatorio-30-g55: Simple vocative: the name 'Dante' addressed directly; marks the addressee of the following imperative statements.
segments.purgatorio-30-g56: 'perché' here gives the reason ('because'). 'Virgilio se ne vada' is a subordinate clause with 'se' = reflexive 'si' and 'ne' = 'away/from here', and 'vada' is the subjunctive of 'andare' (poetic usage). Together 'because Virgil goes away / is going away.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g57: Negative imperative addressed to 'tu': 'non pianger' = 'do not weep'. 'anco' is an archaic form of 'ancora' meaning 'yet' or 'still' (so: 'do not weep yet'). The verb appears in the infinitive/imperative form common in poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-30-g58: A repeated command with the more modern form 'piangere' and 'ancora' ('do not weep now / do not weep again'), reinforcing the injunction; 'non piangere' functions as negative imperative.
segments.purgatorio-30-g59: 'ché' = 'for/because'. The impersonal construction 'pianger ti conven' literally places the infinitive 'pianger' ('to weep') before the clitic 'ti' and the verb 'conven' (from 'convenire' = 'it is fitting/it behooves'): 'it behooves you to weep'. 'per altra spada' ('for another sword') is a metaphorical phrase indicating a different cause or wound for which he must weep. The clause states the reason why he should not yet weep for Virgil.
segments.purgatorio-30-g60: A simile introduced by Quasi ('like/as'), with ammiraglio as the head noun followed by a relative clause (che ... ministra). In poppa e in prora are two prepositional phrases giving position (on the stern and on the prow). Viene a veder is venire + infinitive meaning 'comes to see'; la gente che ministra is a relative clause ('the people who serve/attend'), and per li altri legni means 'among/for the other vessels' (per = among/through).
segments.purgatorio-30-g61: E introduces a further purpose: a + ben + far (infinitive) expresses purpose/intent 'to do well/to set properly'. L’ incora is the object 'the anchor' (poetic form l’ before a vowel); the infinitive far governs that object: 'and to properly make/lay the anchor.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g62: In su = 'upon/on'; la sponda del carro sinistra is a noun phrase 'the left bank/side of the chariot' (del = di + il). Sinistra follows sponda as an adjectival specification ('left side').
segments.purgatorio-30-g63: Quando introduces a temporal clause. Mi volsi is passato remoto (volgersi/voltarsi) 'I turned (myself)'. Al suon del nome mio = 'at the sound of my name', where suon is used concretely for the sounding of the name and the possessive mio follows the noun.
segments.purgatorio-30-g64: Che is a relative pronoun linking back to 'the sound of my name' (or the circumstance just mentioned). Di necessità = 'by necessity' / 'necessarily'. Qui si registra uses si as an impersonal/passive marker and registra (present indicative) 'is recorded/registered here.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g65: Vidi is passato remoto 'I saw.' La donna = 'the lady' (here Beatrice). Che introduces a relative clause; pria m’appario is compressed/poetic: pria = 'earlier/previously', m’appario = mi apparì 'appeared to me' (past), and velata sotto l’angelica festa is a participial phrase 'veiled beneath the angelic festival/feast', modifying la donna.
segments.purgatorio-30-g66: Drizzar is the infinitive 'to raise/straighten' used after vidi to indicate what she did that the narrator saw (common in older/poetic syntax). Li occhi is the direct object 'her eyes'. Ver' (verso) = 'toward', me = 'me'. Di qua dal rio = 'on this side of the stream/river' (di qua = on this side; dal = da + il).
segments.purgatorio-30-g67: Tutto che here functions concessively 'although/even though.' ’L vel (poetic elision of il velo) is the noun 'the veil' and che introduces the relative clause. Le scendea di testa uses scendea (imperfect) 'was descending' and le is the dative/clitic referring to her (i.e. 'from her head'); di testa = 'from/of the head.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g68: Cerchiato is a past participle used adjectivally 'encircled' (i.e. the veil encircled by something). De le fronde di Minerva = 'with the branches/foliage of Minerva' (de = archaic form of di). The phrase specifies the ornament encircling the veil.
segments.purgatorio-30-g69: Non ... lasciasse is in the imperfect subjunctive (lasciasse) within the concessive clause introduced by Tutto che — 'did not allow/permit.' La is the direct object 'her.' Parer manifesta uses parer (infinitive) 'to seem/appear' with manifesta as predicative adjective 'manifest/plain.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g70: Regalmente 'regally/majestically' modifies the action described. Ne l'atto = 'in that act/gesture' (ne = in it/regarding it). Ancor = 'still/yet.' Proterva is an adjective 'overbold/obstinate/forward' describing the gesture. Continüò is passato remoto 'she continued.' Come colui che dice ...: 'like one who says and keeps the warmest part of his speech in reserve' — parlar dietro reserva is a set phrase meaning to hold back the most passionate words.
segments.purgatorio-30-g71: Guardaci is a vocative/imperative form meaning either 'look at us' or 'watch/guard us' (here an exhortation to keep care/watch); ben = 'well' intensifies the imperative: 'Watch over us well' or 'Take good care of us.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g27: This segment is only the closing quotation mark and punctuation indicating the end of the preceding speech; there is no lexical or grammatical material to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-30-g28: Io vidi già nel cominciar del giorno: straightforward subject + verb + temporal phrase. 'Io' (subject) + 'vidi' (preterite: 'I saw') places the action in Dante's past narrative. 'già' = 'already' / 'once' here emphasizing the time; 'nel cominciar del giorno' is a temporal infinitive-phrase ('at the beginning of the day') where 'cominciar' is a truncated infinitive form common in poetry.
segments.purgatorio-30-g29: e l’altro ciel di bel sereno addorno: coordination 'e' links this clause to the previous. 'l’altro ciel' = 'the other sky' (poetic phrasing for a different region/face of the sky). 'di bel sereno addorno' is a prepositional phrase where 'di' + adjective-noun 'bel sereno' ('fair/clear weather') functions as a descriptor and 'addorno' (past participle/adjectival use) means 'adorned' or 'dressed'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g30: e la faccia del sol nascere ombrata: 'e' continues the description; 'la faccia del sol' = 'the face of the sun' (poetic 'sol' = sun). 'nascere' here means 'to rise' (sunrise) and is used in its infinitive form after the noun; 'ombrata' is a past participle used adjectivally: 'shadowed' or 'shaded'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g31: sì che per temperanza di vapori l’occhio la sostenea lunga fïata: 'sì che' = 'so that', introducing a result clause. 'per temperanza di vapori' = 'by/through the tempering/moderation of vapors' (an instrumental phrase explaining cause). 'l’occhio la sostenea' = literally 'the eye sustained it' (imperfect 'sostenea' = 'sustained' / 'kept gazing'), and 'lunga fïata' = 'for a long time' (poetic 'fïata' = 'stretch/space of time').
segments.purgatorio-30-g32: così dentro una nuvola di fiori che da le mani angeliche saliva e ricadeva in giù dentro e di fori: 'così' links the manner; 'dentro una nuvola di fiori' = 'within a cloud of flowers'. 'che da le mani angeliche saliva' is a relative clause: 'that rose from angelic hands' (here 'saliva' is the imperfect of 'salire', poetic form meaning 'rose up'). 'e ricadeva in giù dentro e di fori' continues: 'and fell downward, both within and through openings' — 'ricadeva' imperfect 'fell back/fell', and 'dentro e di fori' uses spatial contrasts 'inward and outward/through openings' (poetic image).
segments.purgatorio-30-g33: sovra candido vel cinta d’uliva donna m’apparve,: 'sovra' = 'above/over'; 'candido vel' is an elliptical poetic phrase: 'candido' = 'white/pure' and 'vel' here functions as the noun 'velo' (veil) in older/poetic usage (so 'a white veil'); 'cinta d’uliva' = 'girded with olive' (an olive-wreath); 'donna m’apparve' = 'a woman appeared to me' (perfect 'apparve' = 'appeared'). Word order places the setting before the appearance for dramatic effect.
segments.purgatorio-30-g34: sotto verde manto vestita di color di fiamma viva.: 'sotto verde manto vestita' = 'dressed beneath a green mantle' (past participle 'vestita' adjectival). 'di color di fiamma viva' specifies the color: 'of the color of living/vivid flame' — 'viva' as adjective intensifies 'fiamma' (bright, vivid), so the whole phrase describes her robe's fiery color.
segments.purgatorio-30-g35: E lo spirito mio,: simple nominative phrase. 'E' connects to the previous narrative; 'lo spirito mio' is the noun phrase subject ('my spirit') with the article before the noun, typical Italian word order.
segments.purgatorio-30-g36: che già cotanto tempo era stato ch’a la sua presenza non era di stupor,: a complex relative clause modifying 'spirito'. 'era stato' is the pluperfect/imperfect sequence ('had been' / 'was for a long time'), and 'ch’a la sua presenza non era di stupor' = 'so that at her presence it was not in a state of astonishment' (the idiom 'di stupor' indicates a state/quality). The clause links duration ('già cotanto tempo') to the spirit's habitual condition.
segments.purgatorio-30-g37: tremando,: present participle/gerund 'trembling' describing the continuing condition of the spirit; Dante stacks participles to list simultaneous states.
segments.purgatorio-30-g38: affranto,: another adjectival past participle 'crushed/overwhelmed' parallel to 'tremando', adding to the description of the spirit's emotional state.
segments.purgatorio-30-g39: sanza de li occhi aver più conoscenza,: 'sanza' = 'without' (archaic form of 'senza'); 'de li occhi aver più conoscenza' = 'having no more recognition in the eyes' i.e. 'without the eyes retaining further recognition/awareness'. 'aver' is the infinitive used after the prepositional construction; 'più' = 'any more'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g40: per occulta virtù che da lei mosse,: 'per' = 'by/through' introduces the causal agent; 'occulta virtù' = 'hidden/secret power' (adjective + noun). 'che da lei mosse' is a relative clause: 'that moved/issued from her' — note 'mosse' here is past of 'muovere' ('caused to move/emanated'), an older poetic past form.
segments.purgatorio-30-g41: d’antico amor sentì la gran potenza.: 'd’antico amor' = 'of ancient/former love' (the prepositional phrase modifies the force being felt). 'sentì' is the perfect of 'sentire' here meaning 'felt'; 'la gran potenza' = 'the great power' (direct object). The clause states the experiential effect on the spirit.
segments.purgatorio-30-g42: Tosto che ne la vista mi percosse l’alta virtù che già m’avea trafitto prima ch’io fuor di püerizia fosse,: 'Tosto che' = 'as soon as'. 'ne la vista mi percosse' = 'it struck me in the sight of it' (perfect 'percosse' = 'struck', with 'mi' indirect object). 'l’alta virtù' is the subject 'the high/lofty power'; the embedded relative 'che già m’avea trafitto' ('which had already pierced me') uses pluperfect 'avea trafitto'. 'prima ch’io fuor di püerizia fosse' uses 'prima che' + imperfect subjunctive 'fosse' to indicate anteriority in past narration: 'before I had left my boyhood.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g43: volsimi a la sinistra col respitto col quale il fantolin corre a la mamma quando ha paura o quando elli è afflitto,: 'volsimi' = 'I turned (myself)' (archaic past form of 'volgersi'). 'a la sinistra' = 'to the left'. 'col respitto' = 'with the start/jump' (here 'respitto' denotes a sudden recoil or start). 'col quale il fantolin corre a la mamma' is a simile: 'with which the little boy runs to his mother' and 'quando ha paura o quando elli è afflitto' explains the boy's motivation. Note some lemmas are archaic or printed oddly (e.g. 'elli' = 'he').
segments.purgatorio-30-g44: per dicere a Virgilio:: purpose-infinitive phrase 'per dicere' = 'in order to say' followed by the indirect object 'a Virgilio' = 'to Virgil'. This marks Dante's immediate intention after turning.
segments.purgatorio-30-g45: ‘Men che dramma di sangue m’è rimaso che non tremi:: direct speech. 'Men che' = 'less than' (used with a measure, here 'dramma' = 'a dram' i.e. a small measure). The construction 'm’è rimaso' = 'remains in me' (m’ = 'mi'); 'che non tremi' is a relative clause with subjunctive feel: 'that does not tremble' — the whole exclamation claims there is scarcely any blood in him that does not tremble (hyperbolic). 'dramma' here is a unit of measure, not 'play'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g46: conosco i segni de l’antica fiamma’.: 'conosco' = 'I recognize/know'; 'i segni' = 'the signs'; 'de l’antica fiamma' = 'of the old/ancient flame' (i.e. marks of former love/passion). This closes the quoted exclamation, asserting Dante's certainty about what the signs mean.
segments.purgatorio-30-g101: This segment is only the closing quotation mark and comma, marking the end of the speech begun in the prior lines; it shows that what follows narrates the speaker's physical reaction immediately after those words.
segments.purgatorio-30-g102: 'lo gel' is the subject ('the frost/chill'); the relative pronoun 'che' introduces the clause describing it. 'm’era' is the clitic 'mi' + imperfect 'era' (mi era = it was to/around me), so the imperfect indicates a past, ongoing state; 'intorno al cor' = 'around the heart' (note the contraction 'al' = a + il). 'ristretto' is a past participle used adjectivally ('contracted/tightened').
segments.purgatorio-30-g103: Grammatically this is a verbless (or nominative-predicate) sequence followed by an archaic first-person verb form: 'spirito e acqua' are the items produced, and 'fessi' functions as a past-tense 1st-person verb meaning roughly 'I became / I was made / I was turned into'. The subject 'I' is implicit; the word order (predicate before verb) and the form 'fessi' are poetical/archaic.
segments.purgatorio-30-g104: The conjunction 'e' links this clause to the previous one. 'con angoscia' is an adverbial phrase ('with anguish'); 'de la bocca e de li occhi' uses archaic 'de' for 'di' and means 'from the mouth and from the eyes' (note 'li' is the older plural article). 'uscì' is the passato remoto (3rd person singular) of 'uscire' meaning 'came out' or 'issued forth'; 'del petto' (= 'out of the chest') names the source. Note that although two things (spirit and water) were named, Dante uses a singular verb here to present the outflow as a single event or mass.
segments.purgatorio-30-g105: Ella is the explicit third-person feminine subject pronoun ‘she’. In Italian narrative the subject is often omitted, so its presence here gives emphasis or clarity; the comma marks a short pause before the following participial phrase describing her posture.
segments.purgatorio-30-g106: This is an adverbial phrase of posture. Pur = still/yet (adds continuity); ferma is an adjectival/participial sense ‘firm, stationary’ describing her position; in su = on/upon (a two-word prepositional expression); la detta coscia del carro = 'the said/thus-mentioned thigh of the chariot' (detta = 'said/aforementioned'); stando is the gerund of stare (‘being/standing’), reinforcing that this is the circumstance in which she speaks.
segments.purgatorio-30-g107: A simple transitive clause: a le = 'to the' (dative), sustanze pie is an old-fashioned expression for the pious beings/souls (literal 'sustances' used as persons), volse is passato remoto (3rd sg) of volgere meaning 'she turned/directed' and takes le sue parole as its object; così poscia = 'thus afterwards/then', introducing the quoted speech.
segments.purgatorio-30-g108: Voi vigilate is 2nd-person-plural present indicative (‘you keep watch’); ne l’etterno die is an archaic locution meaning 'in the eternal day' (ne = in, l’etterno die = 'the eternal day'), with the older spelling etterno and die = 'day' (archaic). The sentence is addressed directly to the pious souls.
segments.purgatorio-30-g109: Sì che introduces a result/circumstance ('so that'); notte né sonno a voi non fura = 'neither night nor sleep are (ever) stolen upon you', where fura is an archaic form of 'furare' ('to steal') used impersonally/poetically; passo che faccia il secol per sue vie is a temporal/relative clause ('whenever/at each step that the age takes along its ways'), with faccia (subjunctive) dependent on passo che to indicate each occurrence.
segments.purgatorio-30-g110: Onde = therefore/wherefore, linking back to the previous clause; la mia risposta è con più cura = 'my answer is given with greater care' (con più cura is an adverbial phrase); che m’intenda colui che di là piagne is a final/purpose clause introduced by che + subjunctive intenda = 'so that he who laments over there may understand me' (colui che di là piagne = 'the one who weeps over there').
segments.purgatorio-30-g111: Perché here functions as a final/resultive conjunction 'so that/in order that'; sia is the present subjunctive of essere ('may be/become'), marking desired/resultant state; colpa e duol d’una misura = 'fault and sorrow of one measure' i.e. that blame and grief be proportioned equally—duol is an archaic noun 'sorrow/pain'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g112: Non pur is a correlative negative emphatic: non pur = 'not only' (literally 'not even'). Per ovra de le rote magne means 'by the work/operation of the great wheels'; ovra is the feminine noun 'work' and de le is the archaic spelling for modern 'delle'. Rote magne = 'great wheels' (celestial spheres), with magne agreeing in gender/number.
segments.purgatorio-30-g113: che introduces a relative clause describing 'le rote magne'. Drizzan is an archaic/poetic present 3rd-person plural (drizzano) meaning 'direct/straighten' or 'guide'. Ciascun seme = 'each seed'; ad alcun fine = 'to some purpose/end' (alcun used here positively = 'some' or 'a certain'). Secondo che introduces the subordinate 'according to' clause (according to how the stars are companions).
segments.purgatorio-30-g114: ma per introduces the contrast 'but by/through'. Larghezza di grazie divine = 'the largeness/abundance of divine graces'; larghezza here means 'abundance' or 'generosity' rather than literal width, and grazie is plural 'graces' (divine gifts).
segments.purgatorio-30-g115: This che begins another relative clause modifying 'grazie divine'. Sì alti vapori = 'such high/lofty vapors'; hanno is present indicative 'they have'. A lor piova uses a poetic expression with piova (forma poetica of piovere) meaning 'rains upon them' or 'fall upon them as rain'; a lor = 'to them/upon them'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g116: che again links a consequence: 'so that'. Nostre viste = 'our sights/visions' (here a noun: our power of seeing or sight), là non van vicine = 'do not go/come near there' — van is the archaic form for vanno. The clause says our eyes/sight do not reach that high (they are not near there).
segments.purgatorio-30-g117: questi is an emphatic demonstrative 'this one/this man' (here referring to the person just described). Fu is simple past 'was'. Ne la sua vita nova = 'in his new life' (ne = in). Virtüalmente is an adverb 'virtually' or 'in effect'—a qualifier meaning 'in practical effect' rather than strictly formal.
segments.purgatorio-30-g118: Ch’ (che) introduces the result clause 'that'. Ogne = old form of 'ogni' = 'every'. Abito destro: abito can mean 'habit' or 'garment/way of life' and destro means 'upright/right' — here 'upright habit/behavior'. The phrasing fatto averebbe is an archaic word order for what modern Italian would render 'avrebbe fatto avere' or more idiomatically 'avrebbe mostrato' — averebbe is the conditional auxiliary 'would have' and fatto is the past participle 'made/done', so overall the clause means 'would have shown/had in him (as) a marvelous proof'. In lui = 'in him'; mirabil prova = 'wondrous proof.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g119: Ma tanto più ... si fa is a comparative-correlative construction: 'But all the more ... the more ... (it becomes)'. Tanto più ... si fa = 'the more ... it becomes'. Maligno e più silvestro = 'more malignant/evil and more wild/rustic'; si fa is reflexive/impersonal 'becomes' (fa from fare used as 'become'). Col is the contraction of con + il 'with the'; col mal seme e non cólto = 'with bad seed and uncultivated'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g120: Quant' here = quanto (elliptical) beginning the correlative 'the more ... the more' construction: 'the more that it has'. Elli is an archaic pronoun 'it/they' referring back to the ground (masculine); ha is present 'has'. Più di buon vigor terrestro = 'more of good terrestrial/earthly vigor' — di introduces the quantity possessed. The whole line completes the comparative: 'the more it has of good earthly vigor.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g1: Quando introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when.' The noun phrase il settentrïon del primo cielo names the northern point of the first heaven; del is the contracted preposition + article 'di + il.' Cielo here refers to the celestial sphere (Dante's ordered heavens).
segments.purgatorio-30-g2: This is a relative clause modifying 'settentrïon.' Né ... né coordinates negatives ('neither ... nor'); mai with the negative yields 'never.' Seppe is the passato remoto of sapere meaning 'knew' or 'experienced.' Orto and occaso are contrasted (rising and setting); che di colpa velo specifies the only 'mist' it knew — 'a veil of guilt.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g3: Another relative clause continuing from the same 'che': faceva is the imperfect of fare, here 'was doing' or 'made' (habitual/state), and refers to the heaven's effect there; ciascun accorto ('each one, mindful/prudent') is the subject of the infinitival/noun phrase di suo dover ('of his own duty'). The structure states that the heaven caused each person to be attentive to his duty.
segments.purgatorio-30-g4: come introduces a simile: 'as' or 'just as.' ’l più basso face is an archaic phrasing meaning 'the lowest (one) does' (face is an older form related to facere/fare). The second half qual temon gira per venire a porto is a descriptive clause 'which the helm (temon) turns so as to come into port' — gira is present 'turns,' per venire 'in order to come,' a maritime comparison.
segments.purgatorio-30-g5: fermo is an adjective/adverb 'firm, fixed'; s’affisse is a pronominal/archaic verb form meaning 'set itself' or 'fixed itself' (Dante often uses pronominal reflexive forms to indicate a state being assumed). The colon marks a pause before the description of the people.
segments.purgatorio-30-g6: Simple noun phrase: the definite article + gente (people) qualified by verace 'true/genuine.' This identifies the group that follows.
segments.purgatorio-30-g7: Venuta is a past participle agreeing with la gente (feminine) meaning 'having come'; prima here means 'before' or 'first,' and tra ’l grifone ed esso 'between the griffin and it' locates them spatially (the griffin is an emblem on the chariot).
segments.purgatorio-30-g8: al carro volse sé: volse is an older past tense of volgere/voltare meaning 'turned' (here rendered 'turned themselves'); sé is the reflexive pronoun 'themselves.' come a sua pace 'as to their own rest/peace' — they turned toward the chariot as toward their place of repose or comfort.
segments.purgatorio-30-g9: Short phrase: e un di loro 'and one of them' prepares the report that follows; di loro is the partitive/genitive 'of them.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g10: quasi da ciel messo is a circumstantial phrase 'as if sent from heaven': quasi 'as if,' da ciel 'from heaven,' messo the past participle of mettere used here in the sense 'sent/placed' — 'as though sent by heaven.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g11: Veni is Latin (imperative or vocative feel) meaning 'Come!' used here as an invoked/quoted word; Dante is quoting a chant — the verb form is a direct vocative/imperative call.
segments.purgatorio-30-g12: sponsa is the vocative/accusative Latin noun 'bride' (here addressed as a figurative bride of God or of the Church), presented alone as the object of the quoted invocation.
segments.purgatorio-30-g13: de Libano 'of/from Lebanon' specifies the source in the quoted phrase; cantando is a present participle/gerund 'singing' describing how the cry was uttered; gridò is passato remoto 'he cried out' or 'he shouted,' and tre volte gives frequency 'three times.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g14: And all the others (tutti li altri) appresso 'after him' or 'close behind' — a short summative clause indicating the rest followed his example.
segments.purgatorio-30-g15: Quali begins a simile 'just as' (literally 'such as'); i beati 'the blessed' is the subject. Al novissimo bando means 'at the last call/summons' (eschatological trumpet), surgeran is the future plural 'they will rise' (surgeranno in modern Italian), and presti (here adverbial) means 'quickly/ready.' Ognun di sua caverna 'each from his own cave/tomb' describes the individual rising.
segments.purgatorio-30-g16: la voce revestita: revestita is a past participle agreeing with voce, literally 'the voice re-clothed' (i.e., renewed or made glorious); alleluiando is a gerund meaning 'chanting/singing Alleluia.' The image is of voices clothed anew in praise.
segments.purgatorio-30-g17: cotali 'such (people)' in su la divina basterna 'up onto the divine basterna' (basterna is an archaic term for a type of litter/chariot). Si levar is a pronominal/intransitive reflexive use meaning 'they rose up,' and the clause continues to say that a hundred of them rose.
segments.purgatorio-30-g18: ad vocem tanti senis 'at the voice of so many elders': ad + accusative 'to/at' marks the stimulus, vocem is 'voice,' tanti 'so many,' and senis (plural) refers to old men/elders whose voice prompts the movement.
segments.purgatorio-30-g19: ministri e messaggier di vita etterna is a nominative apposition describing those who rose: ministri (servants/ministers) and messengers, 'of eternal life' (di vita etterna) explains their mission — they are agents and heralds of everlasting life.
segments.purgatorio-30-g20: Tutti 'all' is the subject and dicean is the imperfect 'they were saying' (habitual/past continuous), introducing the quoted exclamation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-30-g21: A Latin formula: Benedictus 'blessed' (vocable) followed by qui venis 'who comes' (classical element meaning 'blessed is he who comes'). Dante preserves the Latin phrase as the content of their chant.
segments.purgatorio-30-g22: This segment is punctuation marking the end of the previous quotation and the continuation of the narration; there are no lexical items here to gloss.
segments.purgatorio-30-g23: e fior gittando e di sopra e dintorno: a participial phrase 'and throwing flowers, both above and around' — gittando is a gerund 'throwing,' and di sopra / dintorno indicate spatial distribution 'from above' and 'around.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g24: Manibus is Latin ablative plural 'with hands' (here quoted as part of the liturgical/poetic line); Dante reproduces the Latin phrase exactly for effect.
segments.purgatorio-30-g25: oh is an exclamation interjected into the quoted Latin line; it functions as an emotional appeal in the chant.
segments.purgatorio-30-g26: date lilïa plenis is a Latin imperative: date (2nd person plural) 'give,' lilïa 'lilies' (accusative plural), plenis 'full' (ablative plural modifying manibus earlier in the hymn). The construction is a concise liturgical request: 'O hands, give full lilies.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g136: "Tanto giù cadde," places two adverbs before the verb: tanto (an intensifier: "so/so much") + giù (direction "down"). Cadde is the passato remoto, 3rd person singular of cadere, meaning "(he/it) fell"; the subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.purgatorio-30-g137: Che introduces the result clause ("so... that"). Tutti argomenti is the plural noun phrase "all arguments/reasons." A la salute sua is a prepositional phrase meaning "for his salvation" (a + article la). Eran is the imperfect tense (poetic form of erano) 3rd person plural "were," già = "already," and corti is a predicative adjective meaning "insufficient/short" (i.e. not enough).
segments.purgatorio-30-g138: Fuor che is a fixed idiom meaning "except that/except that...". Mostrarli is an infinitive with an enclitic object li ("show them"); in older/poetic Italian an infinitive can stand in for a finite past action here (effectively: "except that the lost people showed them"). Le perdute genti is the subject phrase "the lost people" (le = feminine plural article + perdute = "lost/damned"), who perform the showing.
segments.purgatorio-30-g139: Per questo is a causal phrase "for this/for this reason." Visitai is passato remoto, 1st person singular "I visited." L'uscio d'i morti uses the archaic contraction d'i for di + i ("of the dead"); uscio = "door/threshold."
segments.purgatorio-30-g140: E a colui introduces an indirect object: "and to him." Colui che is a relative phrase "he who/that one who." L' ha condotto is a compound verb: l' (clitic) + ha (present perfect auxiliary "has") + condotto (past participle "led"); qua sù are adverbs "here up" emphasizing the location. The clause means "to him who has brought him up here."
segments.purgatorio-30-g141: Li here is the old plural definite article (equivalent to modern i), so Li prieghi miei is a noun phrase "the prayers of mine" (word order with the possessive after the noun, typical in poetry).
segments.purgatorio-30-g142: Piangendo is the gerund of piangere ("weeping/while weeping") and indicates the manner in which the prayers were offered or brought.
segments.purgatorio-30-g143: Furon is the passato remoto 3rd person plural of essere used here as an auxiliary or copula "they were." Porti is used poetically as a past participle meaning "brought/transported" (here forming a passive sense: "were brought"). Together, furon porti = "were brought/carried."
segments.purgatorio-30-g144: Alto fato di Dio is a noun phrase "a lofty/high decree/fate of God." Sarebbe is the conditional of essere ("would be"). Rotto is a past participle used predicatively: "would be broken/interrupted." The whole clause states a hypothetical consequence.
segments.purgatorio-30-g145: This is the protasis ("if" clause). Se = "if." Letè is the Lethe (river of oblivion); si passasse is the imperfect subjunctive of passare with si reflexive/impersonal usage, meaning roughly "if one were to pass/cross Lethe." E tal vivanda fosse gustata uses fosse gustata (imperfect subjunctive passive) "such food were tasted" to continue the counterfactual condition. Sanza alcuno scotto di pentimento = "without any price/payment (scotto) of repentance," where scotto means a reckoning or price to be paid. Che introduces a relative clause modifying pentimento ("repentance that..."), and spanda is the present subjunctive (3rd person) of spandere "to pour forth," so che lagrime spanda = "which pours forth tears." Grammatically, the two subjunctives (passasse, fosse) mark a hypothetical contrary-to-fact condition tied to the conditional in the main clause.
segments.purgatorio-30-g121: Alcun tempo is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'for some time.' il is the unstressed object pronoun 'him' placed before the verb; sostenni is passato remoto (1st person) of sostenere, here 'I supported/held up.' col is the contraction of con + il 'with the,' and mio volto = 'my face.' The whole clause means 'For a while I supported him with my face.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g122: mostrando is a present gerund ('showing') that expresses an action simultaneous with the main verb; li occhi is the (archaic) article + noun 'the eyes,' giovanetti functions adjectivally as 'young' (a diminutive/affectionate form), a lui marks the indirect object 'to him.' The gerund here means 'showing the youthful eyes to him.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g123: meco is an archaic adverb meaning 'with me.' il is again the unstressed object pronoun 'him' before the verb; menava is the imperfect (or iterative past) of menare 'to lead,' indicating a habitual action ('used to lead'). in dritta parte is a prepositional phrase 'into/ toward the straight (right) side,' and vòlto is a past participle/adjectival 'turned' describing orientation. Together: 'he used to lead him, with me, my face turned toward the straight side.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g124: Sì tosto come is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'as soon as.' in su la soglia fui is a poetic way to say 'I came upon / was on the threshold' (fui = passato remoto 'I was'). di mia seconda etade = 'of my second age' (i.e. a later stage of youth), and mutai vita uses mutai (passato remoto of mutare) with vita as direct object: 'I changed my life / way of life.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g125: questi (an older form of questo) functions here as 'this one' or 'he' (subject). si tolse is the passato remoto of the pronominal verb togliersi ('to withdraw oneself / remove oneself'), with a meaning 'he withdrew (himself) from me'; a me indicates the person affected ('from me' in this context).
segments.purgatorio-30-g126: The e simply links clauses ('and'). diessi is an archaic/contracted form best understood as equivalent to si diede ('he gave himself' / 'devoted himself'); altrui is a dative/adverbial meaning 'to others' or 'to somebody else.' So the clause means 'and he betook himself to others.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g127: Quando is the temporal conjunction 'when.' di carne a spirto is a compact phrase 'from flesh to spirit' indicating a transformation; era salita is the pluperfect/perfect formed with essere + past participle (era salita = 'had risen'), where salita agrees in gender/number with the subject. The clause: 'When she/it had risen from flesh to spirit.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g128: This clause has two subjects coordinated (bellezza e virtù). cresciuta m’era is a compound past (era + cresciuta) meaning 'had grown in me'; m' (mi) is the dative/experiencer clitic 'in/for me.' The sense is 'beauty and virtue had increased in me.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g129: fu' io is a contracted form of fui io (passato remoto of essere) with a slightly emphatic subject pronoun 'I'; a lui is the dative 'to him.' men cara e men gradita uses the adverb men 'less' with two adjectives: cara 'dear' and gradita 'pleasing,' yielding 'I was less dear to him and less pleasing.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g130: e volse is passato remoto of volgere, 'he turned.' i passi suoi = 'his steps' (passi = steps); per via non vera = 'by/on a way not true' i.e. 'along a false road.' The clause means 'and he turned his steps onto an untrue path.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g131: This participial clause modifies the previous action: imagini di ben 'images of good' is the object of seguendo (gerund 'following'), so 'following false images of the good.' Note that imagini here is a plural noun ('images'/'visions'); seguendo links it to the action of turning his steps.
segments.purgatorio-30-g132: che is a relative pronoun 'which' introducing the result clause; nulla promession = 'no promises' (promession is an archaic noun 'promise/vow'); rendono is present indicative of rendere 'they make/render,' and intera 'whole' is the complement: 'which render no promise whole' (i.e., make promises unfulfilled).
segments.purgatorio-30-g133: Né is the negative conjunction 'nor.' l’impetrare is a gerund/nominalized infinitive 'the entreating/obtaining by request,' and ispirazion is the noun 'inspiration.' mi is the dative 'to/for me,' and valse is the passato remoto (or perfect) of valere 'to be of use/avail.' Together: 'Nor did pleading-for inspiration avail me.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g134: con le quali = 'with which' (referring to the inspirations); e in sogno e altrimenti lists the manners 'both in dream and otherwise'; lo rivocai is passato remoto 'I recalled him / called him back,' with lo the direct object pronoun 'him' placed before the verb. The whole clause: 'with which I called him back, both in dream and other ways.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g135: sì poco = 'so little'; a lui = 'to him'; ne is the clitic 'of it / of them' referring back to what was done/recalled; calse is an archaic past form meaning 'it mattered/it was of consequence' (here negative in sense: it mattered very little to him). The exclamatory word order emphasizes the smallness of its effect on him.
segments.purgatorio-30-g77: Li occhi is the full noun phrase subject ('the eyes'); mi is a dative clitic referring to the speaker (literally 'to me' but idiomatically 'my eyes'). Cadder is an archaic literary passato remoto (third person plural) of cadere meaning 'fell' and giù is a directional adverb 'down'. Nel chiaro fonte is a prepositional phrase ('into the clear spring/fountain') indicating where the eyes fell.
segments.purgatorio-30-g78: Ma is the adversative conjunction 'but'. Veggendomi is a present gerund/participle with the enclitic mi ('seeing myself'); it means 'seeing myself' (I saw my own reflection). In esso means 'in it' (the spring), where esso refers back to the fountain.
segments.purgatorio-30-g79: I before the verb is an unstressed direct-object pronoun ('them', i.e. the eyes). Trassi is first‑person singular passato remoto of trarre (here 'I drew' or 'I pulled'); the clitic pronoun appears before the verb as typical in Italian. A l’erba (a + l’ = to the grass) gives the direction/target of the motion: 'to the grass' (i.e. he turned his gaze toward the grass).
segments.purgatorio-30-g80: Tanta vergogna is the subject phrase 'so great a shame'; mi is again a dative clitic meaning 'to me' or 'upon me'. Gravò is passato remoto (third person singular) of gravare meaning 'weighed down' or 'pressed upon'. La fronte is the affected surface ('the brow/forehead'). The construction means 'such shame weighed upon my brow.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g81: Così 'thus' sets up a comparison. La madre is the noun phrase 'the mother'. Al figlio is a dative phrase 'to/for the son' indicating the perspective from which she appears. Par is an archaic present form of parere ('seems') and superba is the predicate adjective 'proud/haughty'. The clause means 'the mother seems proud to her son.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g82: Com' is shortened come 'as' (the apostrophe marks elision). Ella parve is passato remoto (third person singular) of parere: 'she seemed'. A me is the indirect object 'to me' (i.e. 'as she seemed to me'). This clause states the poet's own perception in the past tense.
segments.purgatorio-30-g83: Perché introduces the reason ('because'). D'amaro is the contracted di + amaro ('of/from bitterness' or 'bitterly'); sente is present tense of sentire meaning 'feels/tastes' here. Il sapor is 'the taste/flavour' and de la pietade acerba is a genitive phrase 'of the bitter/harsh pity' (acerba modifies pietade). Altogether: 'because she feels the bitter taste of pity.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g84: Ella 'she' is the subject; si is the reflexive/mediopassive clitic and tacque is passato remoto (third person singular) of tacere meaning 'fell silent' or 'became silent'. The phrase is a simple narrative past: 'She fell silent.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g85: E is 'and'. Li angeli is the subject 'the angels' (li is the archaic definite article used before plural nouns). Cantaro here is an archaic past tense form of cantare meaning 'they sang' (past historic), and di sùbito means 'immediately' or 'at once'. The clause introduces the angels' song; the following words start the quoted liturgical line.
segments.purgatorio-30-g86: Domine is Latin and here vocative ('O Lord') opening the quoted Psalm verse the angels sing. It is left untranslated inside the quotation in the original but functions as address in the Psalm line.
segments.purgatorio-30-g87: Speravi is the Latin perfect form 'I have hoped' (from the Psalm verse 'In te, Domine, speravi'). In the poem it is part of the quoted liturgical text the angels sing, so the verb appears in Latin form rather than translated into Italian.
segments.purgatorio-30-g88: Ma is 'but' introducing contrast. Oltre 'beyond' marks a limit; 'pedes meos' is a Latin phrase ('my feet') taken from the Psalm and quoted as the breakpoint of the singing. Non passaro is an archaic third‑person plural passato remoto of passare ('they did not pass/go beyond'). The clause means the angels' song stopped at the words 'pedes meos' and did not continue.
segments.purgatorio-30-g89: Sì come is a comparative 'just as/like'. Neve is the subject 'snow'; tra le vive travi means 'between the living/green beams' (vive functioning adjectivally). Per lo dosso d’Italia is a locative phrase 'along the ridge of Italy' (dosso = ridge/back). Si congela is a reflexive/impersonal present ('it freezes' or 'it becomes frozen'), a generalizing present used to describe a natural process.
segments.purgatorio-30-g90: Soffiata e stretta are past participles describing the snow: 'blown and pressed/tightened'. Da li venti schiavi is an agent phrase 'by the slavish winds' (li is the archaic plural article, schiavi figuratively 'servile/harsh' or simply 'enslaved' as a poetic epithet). The participles qualify how the snow is compacted by the winds.
segments.purgatorio-30-g91: Poi is a simple temporal adverb 'then' or 'afterwards', linking the previous state to what follows.
segments.purgatorio-30-g92: Liquefatta is the feminine past participle 'liquefied' agreeing with neve (feminine); it denotes the snow once it has melted.
segments.purgatorio-30-g93: In sé stessa ('into/within itself') is an emphatic reflexive phrase; trapela is present indicative 'seeps' or 'filters through/away'. Together they mean the melted snow seeps away into itself/the ground.
segments.purgatorio-30-g94: Pur che here means 'provided that' (a concessive/conditional connector), introducing the condition: la terra che perde ombra spiri — 'the earth/ground that loses its shade breathes'. Spiri is subjunctive present (after pur che) of spirare ('to breathe' or 'to give off a breeze'), a poetically charged image explaining when the melt will seep away.
segments.purgatorio-30-g95: Sì che introduces a result clause 'so that' or 'so that it appears'. Par is present 'seems/appears'. Foco is 'fire' and fonder is the infinitive 'to melt' (here used after par in the sense 'seems to melt'), with la candela as the object: 'it seems the candle melts like a fire.'
segments.purgatorio-30-g96: Così fui is narrative passato remoto 'so I was'. Sanza is archaic for 'without' (modern senza). Lagrime e sospiri are the things he lacked ('tears and sighs'). Anzi introduces a corrective contrast 'rather'; ’l cantar is a nominalized infinitive 'the singing' (the song) of quei che notan sempre dietro a le note de li etterni giri—a relative clause: quei che notan sempre = 'those who always mark/keep (the rhythm)', dietro a le note de li etterni giri = 'behind the notes of the eternal circuits' (i.e. the angelic choir that follows the eternal rounds).
segments.purgatorio-30-g97: Ma poi che = 'but when' or 'however, after'. ’Ntesi is passato remoto (first person singular) of intendere/ntendere (here 'I heard' or 'I perceived'); ne le dolci tempre = 'in their sweet tones/timbres' (tempre = timbres/tones, a poetic noun). Lor compatire a me = 'their pity/compassion toward me' (compatire used as infinitive/noun phrase). The line means: 'but when I heard in their sweet tones their compassion for me...'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g98: Par che introduces a hypothetical/simulative clause 'it seems that/as if'. Se is an archaic reflexive/particle here (functioning like modern si in 'si fosseno detti'); detto is the past participle 'said'. Avesser is an archaic auxiliary form (a literary subjunctive/pluperfect construction meaning 'they had' / 'they would have had') used to form the pluperfect hypothetical — overall: 'it seems as if they had said'.
segments.purgatorio-30-g99: Donna is a vocative 'Lady' (addressing the woman, Beatrice). In Dante it is a respectful/formal mode of address rather than a plain noun.
segments.purgatorio-30-g100: Perché introduces a direct question 'why'. Sì 'so/thus' intensifies the following phrase. Lo stempre is a compacted older expression meaning 'ever thus / always so' (stempre = sempre/always; lo is a neuter pronoun/particle used idiomatically). The whole quoted question asks, roughly, 'Lady, why are you ever thus?'

sentences.purgatorio-30-s14: How did you deign to come up the mountain?
sentences.purgatorio-30-s15: Didn't you know that here is the happy (or blessed) man?
sentences.purgatorio-30-s9: But Virgil had left them bereft of himself — Virgil, most gentle father, Virgil who gave himself for my salvation; nor, however much I had lost my ancient mother, did that avail to the cheeks pure with dew, that, weeping, would not turn dark.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s10: “Dante, because Virgil is leaving, do not weep yet, do not weep now; for you must weep on account of another sword.”
sentences.purgatorio-30-s11: Like an admiral who, from stern and prow, comes to look at the people who attend to the other ships, and to properly heave the anchor; upon the left side of the chariot, when I turned at the sound of my name — which of necessity is noted here — I saw the lady who had earlier appeared to me, veiled beneath the angelic festival, raise her eyes toward me on this side of the stream.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s12: Although the veil that descended from her head, girded with Minerva’s leaves, did not allow her to appear plainly, she nevertheless continued regally in that proud gesture, like one who speaks but keeps the warmer part of his speech in reserve: "Take good care of us!"
sentences.purgatorio-30-s6: I once saw, at the beginning of the day, the whole eastern part all rosy, and the other sky adorned with fair clear weather; and the face of the sun rising was shaded, so that, by the tempering of vapors, the eye sustained it for a long time: thus, within a cloud of flowers that rose from angelic hands and fell down both inward and outward, above a white veil girded with olive a woman appeared to me, clad beneath a green mantle in the color of living flame.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s7: And my spirit, which had for so long a time been such that at her presence it was not in a state of astonishment — trembling, crushed, without the eyes retaining further recognition — by an occult virtue that moved from her felt the great power of ancient love.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s8: No sooner had that high virtue struck me in my sight — the same power that had already pierced me before I left boyhood — than I turned to the left with the start with which a little boy runs to his mother when he is afraid or distressed, to say to Virgil: 'There remains less than a dram of blood in me that does not tremble: I know the signs of the ancient flame.'
sentences.purgatorio-30-s21: The ice that had tightened around my heart turned into spirit and water, and with anguish it came out of my chest through my mouth and my eyes.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s22: She, remaining firmly upon the said thigh of the chariot, standing there, then turned her words to the pious souls and said as follows: “You keep watch in the eternal day, so that neither night nor sleep are ever stolen upon you when the age makes a turn along its ways; therefore my answer is given with greater care so that he who laments over there may understand me, that fault and sorrow be of one measure.”
sentences.purgatorio-30-s23: Not only by the working of the great wheels, which steer every seed to some end according to the companionship of the stars, but by the abundance of divine graces — which send down such high vapors upon them that our sight does not reach near there — he was such, in his new life, virtually, that every right habit would have shown in him a marvelous proof.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s24: But the ground becomes all the more malignant and more wild with bad seed and uncultivated, the more it has of good earthly vigor.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s1: When the north of the first heaven, which never knew either setting or rising nor any other fog than a veil of guilt, and which there made every one heedful of his duty, as the lowest (sail) behaves when the helm is put about to make for port, fixed itself firm: the true folk, having come first between the griffin and it, turned themselves toward the chariot as to their resting-place; and one of them, as if sent from heaven, cried, singing “Veni, sponsa de Libano,” three times, and all the others after him.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s2: Just as the blessed at the last summons will quickly rise, each from his own cavern — the voice re-clothed, chanting 'Alleluia' — so a hundred such rose up upon the divine litter at the voice of so many elders, ministers and messengers of eternal life.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s4: and throwing flowers both above and around, 'Manibus, oh, date lilïa plenis!'
sentences.purgatorio-30-s29: He fell so far down that all arguments for his salvation were already inadequate, except that the lost people showed them.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s30: For this reason I visited the door of the dead, and my prayers, weeping, were brought to him who has led him up here.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s31: God's lofty decree would be broken if Lethe were crossed and such food were tasted without any price of repentance that pours forth tears.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s25: For a time I upheld him with my face: showing my youthful eyes to him, he used to lead him with me, my face turned toward the straight side.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s26: As soon as I had set foot upon the threshold of my second age and changed my life, he withdrew from me and gave himself to others.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s27: When she had risen from flesh to spirit, and beauty and virtue had grown in me, I became less dear and less pleasing to him; and he turned his steps onto an untrue path, following false images of good, which make no promise whole.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s28: Nor did entreating inspiration avail me; with which I recalled him, both in dream and otherwise: it mattered so little to him!
sentences.purgatorio-30-s17: My eyes dropped into the clear spring; but seeing myself in it, I drew them to the grass, so much shame weighed on my brow.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s18: Thus the mother seems proud to her son, as she seemed to me; for she tastes the bitter flavour of pity.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s19: She fell silent; and the angels immediately sang 'In te, Domine, speravi'; but they did not go beyond 'pedes meos'.
sentences.purgatorio-30-s20: Just as snow freezes between the living beams along the ridge of Italy, blown and pressed by the slavish winds, and then, once liquefied, seeps away into itself—provided that the land that loses its shade breathes, so that the candle seems to melt like a flame—so I was without tears and sighs, rather with the singing of those who are ever setting the beat behind the notes of the eternal rounds; but when I heard in their sweet tones their compassion for me, it seems as if they had said: 'Lady, why are you ever thus?'
